name: Raiju
description: Raiju (雷獣, "thunder beast" or "thunder animal") is a legendary creature from Japanese mythology. Its body is composed of pure lightning and is typically described as being in the form of a cat, dog, monkey, weasel, or even a blue wolf wrapped in lightning. Raiju is the companion of Raijin, the Shinto god of thunder, and is said to leap from tree to tree during thunderstorms. When thunder strikes, it is said that Raiju has fallen to earth. While generally harmless, Raiju can sometimes cause mischief or destruction. A common belief is that Raiju sleeps in human navels during storms, prompting Raijin to shoot arrows at the navels to wake the Raiju and cause thunder. People who sleep on their stomachs are therefore considered safe from Raiju. Variations in depiction exist across different regions of Japan, with some accounts describing it as having claws and sharp teeth, while others depict it as a more benevolent spirit. It is a powerful symbol of the raw energy and unpredictable nature of thunderstorms.
subtitle: Thunder Beast, Raijin's Companion
appearance: Raiju's appearance varies depending on the region and telling, but it often takes the form of a mammal enveloped in electricity. Common forms include a cat, dog, monkey, weasel, or blue wolf. Its body glows with intense light, and sparks crackle around it. Some descriptions include sharp claws and teeth, while others depict a softer, more approachable visage. Regardless of its specific form, Raiju is always distinctly associated with lightning and thunder.
habitat: Raiju resides in the sky, traveling with thunderstorms. It leaps from tree to tree and can be found wherever lightning strikes are common. During lulls in storms, it may seek shelter in tall trees or even, according to legend, human navels.
behavior: Raiju is generally considered a mischievous but not malicious creature. It follows Raijin, the thunder god, and its movements are linked to the occurrence of thunder and lightning. While usually harmless, Raiju can sometimes cause accidental damage due to its electrical nature. It is said to be playful and enjoys the energy of storms.

keyMyths.title: Raiju in the Navel
keyMyths.description: It is said that during thunderstorms, Raiju sometimes sleeps in human navels. Raijin shoots arrows at the navels to wake the Raiju, causing thunder. Sleeping on one's stomach is believed to prevent this.

sources.author: Basil Hall Chamberlain
sources.description: Early descriptions of Japanese folklore, including Raiju.
sources.text: Early descriptions of Japanese folklore, including Raiju.

sources.author: Multiple
sources.description: Collection of folklore tales containing descriptions of Raiju.
